title: How the CIA Secretly Funded Abstract Expressionism During the Cold War
description: ConôÙsidôÙerôÙing the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of a truôÙly proôÙleôÙtarôÙiôÙan art, the great EngôÙlish litôÙerôÙary critôÙic William EmpôÙson once wrote, ãthe reaôÙso
body:
ConôÙsidôÙerôÙing the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of a truôÙly proôÙleôÙtarôÙiôÙan art, the great EngôÙlish litôÙerôÙary critôÙic William EmpôÙson once wrote, ãthe reaôÙson an EngôÙlish audiôÙence can enjoy RussôÙian proôÙpaôÙganôÙdist films is that the proôÙpaôÙganôÙda is too remote to be annoyôÙing.ã PerôÙhaps this is why AmerôÙiôÙcan artists and bohemiôÙans have so often takôÙen to the politôÙiôÙcal iconogôÙraôÙphy of far-flung regimes, in ways both romanôÙtic and ironôÙic. One nationãs tedious socialôÙist realôÙism is anotherãs radôÙiôÙcal exotôÙiôÙca.
But do U.S. culôÙturôÙal exports have the same effect? One need only look at the sucôÙcess of our most banal brandôÙing overôÙseas to answer in the affirôÙmaôÙtive. Yet no one would think to add Abstract ExpresôÙsionôÙist paintôÙing to a list that includes fast food and Walt DisôÙney prodôÙucts.
NevôÙerôÙtheôÙless, the work of such artists as JackôÙson PolôÙlock, Mark Rothko, and Willem de KoonôÙing wound up as part of a secret CIA proôÙgram durôÙing the height of the Cold War, aimed at proôÙmotôÙing AmerôÙiôÙcan ideals abroad.

The artists themôÙselves were comôÙpleteôÙly unaware that their work was being used as proôÙpaôÙganôÙda. On what agents called a ãlong leash,ã they parôÙticôÙiôÙpatôÙed in sevôÙerôÙal exhiôÙbiôÙtions secretôÙly orgaôÙnized by the CIA, such as ãThe New AmerôÙiôÙcan PaintôÙingã (see catôÙaôÙlog covôÙer at top), which visôÙitôÙed major EuroôÙpean cities in 1958ã59 and includôÙed such modôÙern primôÙiôÙtive works as surôÙreôÙalôÙistô William Baziotesã 1947ô Dwarfô (below) and 1951ãsô TourôÙnaôÙmentô byô Adolphô GotôÙtlieb above.

Of course what seems most bizarre about this turn of events is that avant-garde art in AmerôÙiôÙca has nevôÙer been much appreôÙciôÙatôÙed by the averôÙage citôÙiôÙzen, to put it mildôÙly. AmerôÙiôÙcan Main Streets harôÙbor underôÙcurôÙrents of disôÙtrust or outôÙright hatred for out-there, art-world experôÙiôÙmenôÙtaôÙtion, a trend that filôÙters upward and periôÙodôÙiôÙcalôÙly erupts in conôÙtroôÙverôÙsies over ConôÙgresôÙsionôÙal fundôÙing for the arts. A 1995ô IndeôÙpenôÙdent artiôÙcleô on the CIAãs role in proôÙmotôÙing Abstract ExpresôÙsionôÙism describes these attiôÙtudes durôÙing the Cold War periôÙod:
In the 1950s and 1960sãÎ the great majorôÙiôÙty of AmerôÙiôÙcans disôÙliked or even despised modôÙern artãPresident TruôÙman summed up the popôÙuôÙlar view when he said: ãIf thatãs art, then Iãm a HotôÙtenôÙtot.ã As for the artists themôÙselves, many were ex- comôÙmuôÙnists bareôÙly acceptôÙable in the AmerôÙiôÙca of the McCarthyite era, and cerôÙtainôÙly not the sort of peoôÙple norôÙmalôÙly likeôÙly to receive US govôÙernôÙment backôÙing.
Why, then, did they receive such backôÙing? One short answer:
This philisôÙtinôÙism, comôÙbined with Joseph McCarthyãs hysôÙterôÙiôÙcal denunôÙciôÙaôÙtions of all that was avant-garde or unorthoôÙdox, was deeply embarôÙrassôÙing. It disôÙcredôÙitôÙed the idea that AmerôÙiôÙca was a sophisôÙtiôÙcatôÙed, culôÙturôÙalôÙly rich democôÙraôÙcy.
The one-way relaôÙtionôÙship between modôÙernist painters and the CIAãonly recentôÙly conôÙfirmed by forôÙmer case offiôÙcer DonôÙald Jamesonãsupposedly enabled the agency to make the work of SoviôÙet SocialôÙist RealôÙists appear, in Jamesonãs words, ãeven more stylôÙized and more rigid and conôÙfined than it was.ã (See Evdokiya Usikovaãs 1959 Lenin with VilôÙlagers below, for examôÙple).ô For a longer explaôÙnaôÙtion, read the full artiôÙcle atô The IndeôÙpenôÙdent. Itãs the kind of stoôÙry Don DeLilôÙlo would cook up.

William EmpôÙson goes on to say that ãa Tory audiôÙence subôÙjectôÙed to Tory proôÙpaôÙganôÙda of the same intenôÙsiôÙtyã as RussôÙian imports, ãwould be extremeôÙly bored.ã If he is corôÙrect, itãs likeôÙly that the averôÙage true believôÙer socialôÙist in Europe was already bored silôÙly by SoviôÙet-approved art. What surôÙprisôÙes in these revôÙeôÙlaôÙtions is that the avant-garde works that so radôÙiôÙcalôÙly altered the AmerôÙiôÙcan art world and enraged the averôÙage conôÙgressôÙman and taxôÙpayôÙer were co-optôÙed and colôÙlectôÙed by suave U.S. intelôÙliôÙgence offiôÙcers like so many ShepôÙard Fairey posters.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2013.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
When the State DepartôÙment Used Dizzy GilleôÙspie and Jazz to Fight the Cold War (1956)
DisôÙcovôÙer the CIAãsô SimôÙple SabôÙoôÙtage Field ManôÙuôÙal: A TimeôÙless Guide to SubôÙvertôÙing Any OrgaôÙniôÙzaôÙtion with ãPurôÙposeôÙful StuôÙpidôÙiôÙtyã (1944)
How ãAmericaãs First Drug Czarã Waged War Against BilôÙlie HolôÙiôÙday and OthôÙer Jazz LegôÙends
How the CIA SecretôÙly Used JackôÙson PolôÙlock & OthôÙer Abstract ExpresôÙsionôÙists to Fight the Cold War
The CIAãs Style ManôÙuôÙal & Writerãs Guide: 185 Pages of Tips for WritôÙing Like a Spook
How the CIA FundôÙed & SupôÙportôÙed LitôÙerôÙary MagôÙaôÙzines WorldôÙwide While WagôÙing CulôÙturôÙal War Against ComôÙmuôÙnism
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in WashôÙingôÙton, DC.ô

title: Discover Gadsby: The 50,000-Word Novel Written Without Using the Letter E (1939)
description: ãIf Youth, throughôÙout all hisôÙtoôÙry, had had a chamôÙpiôÙon to stand up for it; to show a doubtôÙing world that a child can think; and, posôÙsiôÙ
body:
ãIf Youth, throughôÙout all hisôÙtoôÙry, had had a chamôÙpiôÙon to stand up for it; to show a doubtôÙing world that a child can think; and, posôÙsiôÙbly, do it pracôÙtiôÙcalôÙly; you wouldôÙnãt conôÙstantôÙly run across folks today who claim that ãa child donãt know anyôÙthing.ãô ã Ranked alongôÙside the othôÙer notable openôÙing senôÙtences of AmerôÙiôÙcan litôÙerôÙaôÙture, this falls someôÙwhat short of, say, ãCall me IshôÙmael.ã The entire novôÙel that folôÙlows is writôÙten in the same oddôÙly stiltôÙed, cirôÙcumôÙlocôÙuôÙtive prose, and a readôÙer who skips the authorãs introôÙducôÙtion may not perôÙceive just what has set it askew for some time. Theyãd also have to be readôÙing an ediôÙtion othôÙer than the first, with its bold promise of a ã50,000 WORD NOVEL WITHOUT THE LETTER ãE.ãô ã
The book is Ernest VinôÙcent Wrightãsô GadsôÙby (1939). Though self-pubôÙlished in the late nineôÙteen-thirôÙties to no fanôÙfare, itãs now acknowlôÙedged more or less wideôÙly as a litôÙerôÙary oddôÙiôÙty, far more often citôÙed as a piece of trivôÙia than actuôÙalôÙly read. (I first learned of it from a list of fun facts on the back of a cereôÙal box, which, lookôÙing back now, seems culôÙturôÙalôÙly approôÙpriôÙate.) As the DisôÙamôÙbi video above explains, in denyôÙing himôÙself e, the sinôÙgle most comôÙmon letôÙter in the EngôÙlish lanôÙguage, Wright denied himôÙselfô the, as well as ãthe majorôÙiôÙty of proôÙnouns, likeô he,ô she,ô they,ô them,ô theirs,ã and so on. ãPast-tense words that useô -ed are out of the quesôÙtion, as is any numôÙber between six and thirôÙty.ã
To some, more surôÙprisôÙing than the fact that Wright manôÙaged to comôÙpose a full-length novôÙel this way (overôÙlookôÙing three thes and an offiôÙcer that slipped into the iniôÙtial print run) is the nature of the stoôÙry he chanôÙneled this conôÙsidôÙerôÙable effort into telling. John GadsôÙby ã not to be conôÙfused with the simôÙiôÙlarôÙly named, much more famous title charôÙacôÙter of anothôÙer novôÙel from the preôÙviôÙous decade ã returns in midôÙdle age to his homeôÙtown of BranôÙton Hills, which has slid into a state of advanced disôÙsoôÙluôÙtion. In despair, he assemôÙbles a youth league dedôÙiôÙcatôÙed to breathôÙing life back into the place, and before those 50,000 very nearôÙly eãless words have passed, the popôÙuôÙlaôÙtion has grown thirôÙtyôÙfold, and heãs become the mayôÙor.
In truth, AmerôÙiôÙcan litôÙerôÙaôÙture of the earôÙly twenôÙtiôÙeth cenôÙtuôÙry is litôÙtered withô GadsôÙbys; itãs just thatô none of the authors of those forôÙgotôÙten homôÙiôÙlies on civic-mindôÙed boosôÙtôÙerôÙism thought to use so strikôÙing a gimôÙmick. TechôÙniôÙcalôÙly called a lipogram, the techôÙnique of omitôÙting a parôÙticôÙuôÙlar letôÙter has since been used since to greater litôÙerôÙary effect. With their charôÙacôÙterôÙisôÙtic weakôÙness for AmerôÙiôÙcan eccenôÙtricôÙiôÙty, cerôÙtain French intelôÙlecôÙtuôÙals evenôÙtuôÙalôÙly took up GadsôÙby as a kind of modôÙel. In 1969, Georges Perec pubôÙlished the longer but simôÙiôÙlarôÙly eãless La DisôÙpariôÙtion, which would have been much more chalôÙlengôÙing to write, givôÙen the French lanôÙguageãs even greater reliance on that missôÙing vowôÙel. Far from a parôÙlor trick, its lipogram resôÙonates with both the conôÙtent of the stoôÙry and sense of absence felt by the author, whoãd lost both parôÙents in World War II. As for this post, perôÙhaps youãve noticed that itãs been writôÙten thus far withôÙout a sinôÙgle instance of the letôÙter z. Please clap.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:
How to JumpôÙstart Your CreôÙative Process with William S. BurôÙroughsã Cut-Up TechôÙnique
An IntroôÙducôÙtion to the Codex SeraphiniôÙanus, the Strangest Book Ever PubôÙlished
The Strangest Books in the World: DisôÙcovôÙer The Madmanãs Library, a CapôÙtiôÙvatôÙing ComôÙpendiôÙum of PecuôÙliar Books & ManôÙuôÙscripts
AmerôÙiôÙcan LitôÙerôÙaôÙture, From the BeginôÙnings to the CivôÙil War: A Free Online Course from NYU
The AmerôÙiôÙcan NovôÙel Since 1945: A Free Yale Course on NovôÙels by Nabokov, KerôÙouac, MorôÙriôÙson, PynôÙchon & More
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: John Cageãs Silent, Avant-Garde Piece 4ãý33ã° Gets Covered by a Death Metal Band
description:
in Music |   	April 7th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When we think of silence, we think of medôÙiôÙtaôÙtive stretchôÙes of calm: hikes through deser
body:
in Music |   	April 7th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

When we think of silence, we think of medôÙiôÙtaôÙtive stretchôÙes of calm: hikes through desertôÙed forôÙestô paths, an earôÙly mornôÙing sunôÙset before the world awakes, a stayôÙcaôÙtion at home with a good book. But we know othôÙer silences: awkôÙward silences, omiôÙnous silences, andãin the case of John Cageãs infaôÙmous conôÙcepôÙtuôÙal piece 4ã33ããa mysôÙtiôÙfyôÙing silence that asks us to lisôÙten, not to nothôÙing, but to everyôÙthing. Instead of focusôÙing our aurôÙal attenôÙtion, Cageãs forôÙmalôÙized exerôÙcise in lisôÙtenôÙing disôÙpersôÙes it, to the nerôÙvous coughs and squeakôÙing shoes of a restôÙless audiôÙence, the ceaseôÙless ebb and flow of trafôÙfic and breathôÙing, the ambiôÙent white noise of heatôÙing and ACãÎ
and the susôÙpendôÙed black noise of death metôÙalãÎ.
Weãre used to seeôÙing 4ã33ã ãperôÙformedã as a clasôÙsiôÙcal exerôÙcise, with a digôÙniôÙfied pianist seatôÙed at the bench, ostenôÙtaôÙtiousôÙly turnôÙing the pages of Cageãs ãscore.ã But thereãs no reaôÙson at all the exerciseãor hoax, some insistãcanãt work in any genre, includôÙing metôÙal. NPRãs All Songs TV brings us the video above, in which ã64 years after its debut perôÙforôÙmance by pianist David Tudor,ã death metôÙal band Dead TerôÙriôÙtoôÙry lines behind their instruôÙments, tunes up, and takes on Cage: ãThereãs a setôÙup, earplugs go in, a brief guiôÙtar chug, a drum-stick count-off andãÎ silence.ã
As in every perôÙforôÙmance of 4ã33ã, weãre drawn not only to what we hear, in this case the sounds inô whatôÙevôÙer room we watch theô video, but also to what we see. And watchôÙing these fiveô metôÙalôÙheads, who are so used to delivôÙerôÙing a conôÙtinôÙuôÙous assault, nod their heads solemnôÙly in silence for over four minôÙutes adds yet anothôÙer interôÙpreôÙtive layôÙer to Cageãs experôÙiôÙment, askôÙing us to conôÙsidôÙer the perôÙforôÙmaôÙtive avant-garde as a domain fit not only for rarôÙiôÙfied clasôÙsiôÙcal and art house audiôÙences but for everyôÙone and anyôÙone.
Also, despite their seriôÙousôÙness, NPR reminds us that Dead Territoryãs take is ãanothôÙer in a long line of 4ãý33ã° perôÙforôÙmances that underôÙstand Cage had a sense of humor while expandôÙing our musiôÙcal uniôÙverse.ã Cageô hapôÙpiôÙly gave his experôÙiôÙments to the world to adapt and improôÙvise as itô sees fit, andãas we see in his own perôÙforôÙmance of 4ã33ã in HarôÙvard Squareãhe was hapôÙpy to make his own changes to silence as well.
Note: An earôÙliôÙer verôÙsion of this post appeared on our site in 2016.
RelatôÙed ConôÙtent:

The CuriôÙous Score for John Cageãs ãSilentã Zen ComôÙpoôÙsiôÙtionô 4ãý33ã°
When the Berlin PhilôÙharôÙmonôÙic PerôÙformed John Cageãs IconôÙic Piece 4ãý33ã°, CapôÙturôÙing the SoliôÙtude of the PanôÙdemôÙic (2020)
Josh Jones is a writer and musiôÙcian based in Durham, NC.ô

title: The Self-Balancing Monorail: A 1910 Train That Could Balance Without Falling
description:
in History, Technology |   	April 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

If monoôÙrails have a bad name,ô The SimpôÙsons may be to blame. In an episode a
body:
in History, Technology |   	April 8th, 2026  Leave a Comment
	
			

	

If monoôÙrails have a bad name,ô The SimpôÙsons may be to blame. In an episode acclaimed for its hilarôÙiôÙousôÙness since it first aired 33 years ago, a huckôÙster shows up in SpringôÙfield and conôÙvinces the town to build just such a tranôÙsit sysôÙtem, which turns out to be not just susôÙpiôÙciousôÙly unnecôÙesôÙsary (at least in young Lisaãs judgôÙment) but also danôÙgerôÙousôÙly shodôÙdy. I watched it while growôÙing up in the subôÙurbs of SeatôÙtle, a city that endured bitôÙterôÙly proôÙtractôÙed conôÙtention over whether or not to build out its own rudiôÙmenôÙtaôÙry monoôÙrail sysôÙtem ã a Worldãs Fair artiôÙfact, like the Space NeeôÙdle ã but finalôÙly optôÙed not to. ConôÙcerns were perôÙpetôÙuôÙalôÙly raised, rightôÙly or wrongôÙly, about the noise and darkôÙness that could result from extendôÙing the wide eleôÙvatôÙed track on which it ran.
But what if there were anothôÙer way to build a monoôÙrail? Indeed, what if it could run on the ground, like a traôÙdiôÙtionôÙal two-railed train? Such was the idea in the head of the indeôÙfatiôÙgaôÙble Irish-AusôÙtralian engiôÙneer Louis BrenôÙnan, whoãs rememôÙbered today for inventôÙing a wire-guidôÙed torôÙpeôÙdo back in 1877.
If things had gone difôÙferôÙentôÙly, maybe heãd be betôÙter rememôÙbered for inventôÙing the gyro monoôÙrail, the subôÙject of the PriôÙmal Space video above. In BrenôÙnanãs design, which he actuôÙalôÙly got built and workôÙing, the car balôÙances on a sinôÙgle rail with the aid of a pair of spinôÙning powôÙered gyroôÙscopes that preôÙvent it from falling over (and, in the case of powôÙer loss, could keep spinôÙning for half an hour to allow a safe evacôÙuôÙaôÙtion), allowôÙing it toô run faster and corôÙner more tightôÙly than the trains the world knew.


	

BrenôÙnanãs gyro monoôÙrail made its pubôÙlic debut at the Japan-British ExhiôÙbiôÙtion in LonôÙdon in 1910, givôÙing 50 pasôÙsenôÙgers at a time the opporôÙtuôÙniôÙty to ride around in a cirôÙcle at 20 miles per hour. Though the interôÙest it drew inspired a minor boom of gyro-staôÙbiôÙlized chilôÙdrenãs toys, it nevôÙer actuôÙalôÙly transôÙlatôÙed into a real tranôÙsit sysôÙtem. Around the same time, a group in GerôÙmany also unveiled their own verôÙsion, and in the decades thereôÙafter, addiôÙtionôÙal abortive efforts were made in RusôÙsia. The engiôÙneerôÙing involved was impresôÙsive, as the video explains, but also a bit too comôÙpliôÙcatôÙed and expenôÙsive for its time. The develôÙopôÙment of a new GerôÙman app-ordered autonomous gyro monoôÙrail sysôÙtem was announced just a few years ago. GivôÙen the posôÙsiôÙbilôÙiôÙty of its enterôÙing proôÙducôÙtion as soon as 2032, we could soon be hearôÙing choôÙrusôÙes of ãMonoôÙrail, monoôÙrail, monoôÙrailã ãô or rather,ô ãMonoôÙcab, MonoôÙcab, MonoôÙcabã ã once again.
RelatôÙed conôÙtent:
Trips on the Worldãs OldôÙest ElecôÙtric SusôÙpenôÙsion RailôÙway in 1902 & 2015 Show How a City Changes Over a CenôÙtuôÙry
Paris Had a MovôÙing SideôÙwalk in 1900, and a Thomas EdiôÙson Film CapôÙtured It in Action
A SubôÙway Ride Through New York City: Watch VinôÙtage Footage from 1905
Why PubôÙlic TranôÙsit Sucks in the UnitôÙed States: Four Videos Tell the StoôÙry
A HarôÙrowôÙing Test DriôÙve of BuckôÙminôÙster Fullerãs 1933 DymaxôÙion Car: Art That Is Scary to Ride
Based in Seoul,ô ColôÙinô Marshallô writes and broadôÙcasts on cities, lanôÙguage, and culôÙture. Heãs the author of the newsletôÙterô Books on Citiesô as well as the booksô ÚõçÙ šš§ õ¡šÏô (No SumôÙmaôÙrizôÙing Korea) andô KoreôÙan Newtro.ô FolôÙlow him on the social netôÙwork forôÙmerôÙly known as TwitôÙter atô @colinmarshall.

title: Colossal Members Have Funded 100 Projects in K-12 Classrooms through DonorsChoose
description: Colossal Members have helped us reach fantastic milestone! Weãre delighted to share that this month, weãve officially assisted us in funding 100 p
body:
Colossal Members have helped us reach fantastic milestone! Weãre delighted to share that this month, weãve officially assisted us in funding 100 projects in classrooms around the nation via DonorsChoose. These include supplies and materials for K-12 students, some of whom are learning about and experiencing art for the first time.
A portion of all Membership fees are allocated to this initiative, and so far weãve been able to contribute more than $13,000, making a substantial difference in numerous learning spaces. And since weãre based in Chicago, we especially like to support classrooms here at home. Hereãs what a few recent recipients had to say after their projects were funded:

ãThese supplies will boost morale and prove to students they are seen and cared about. In a time when it is an act of bravery for them to leave their homes and be in school, the ability to be creative without any limits to materials means more than anything.ã ãMs. Resnick, Gage Park High School, Chicago
ãThank you so much for your generous support of our 4th-grade classroom! Because of you, every student now has a set of vibrant watercolor paints to bring their learning to life.ã ãMrs. Wilson, Dawes Elementary School, Chicago
ãOn behalf of our students, I want to extend a heartfelt thank you for your generous contribution to our art classroom. Your support has made a real and lasting impact ã from sketch books to erasers to our art drying rack ã every supply you helped provide has become a tool for creativity, self-expression, and learning.ã ãMs. Pogue, Deneen School of Excellence, Chicago

Find out more about the specific projects funded onô DonorsChoose, and join us by becoming aô Colossal Member.

title: A Parade of Floating Artworks Honors Hieronymus Bosch in the Netherlands
description: Nothing and nobody is perfect. Imperfections can be found everywhere. From June 18 to 21, experience how these defects and shortcomings, these imp
body:
Nothing and nobody is perfect. Imperfections can be found everywhere. From June 18 to 21, experience how these defects and shortcomings, these imperfections and flaws, can lead to fascinating discoveries and beautiful creations at the 2026 Bosch Parade. This editionãs theme, Powered by Defects, pays a contemporary tribute to the large and small wrongdoings in the world.ô 
Dedicated to painter Hieronymus Bosch (1450-1516), the biannual parade honors the artistãs fascination with the fantastical and absurd. Bosch is known for his symbolic paintings, often tying in gruesome representations of the afterlife and human desire and fear. He is also regarded as one of the earliest genre painters, depicting common people and their everyday experiences.ô 
Skypunch Collective (NL/CH/IT), ãNAûAD.ã Photo by Jona Harnischmacher
Participating artists embody his philosophy and aesthetic with floating artworks uniquely created for the parade. This year, an international fleet of nineteen new creations will drift down the Dommel river at the centre of ãs-Hertogenbosch, the city where the artist was born and that eventually adopted his name.
At the center of the Bosch Parade is the Garden of Earthly Delights: a summery open-air studio on the waterfront at the Citadel, the medieval fortress in the heart of ãs-Hertogenbosch. In this bustling incubator, the public can see and meet the artists bringing their artworks to life.
Since 2010, the Bosch Parade has drawn thousands of visitors to the region. The 2026 edition is curated by David Bade, a multidisciplinary artist and co-founder of the Instituto Buena Bista in CuraûÏao. For the 2024 parade, he created the large, groundbreaking community project, Your Contemporary Demons.
To learn more, visit boschparade.nl/en/, and view the full 2026 program.
Rob van Dam (NL), ãZZNAKEã; Mieke Goris, Geert Verbist, and Bart Vankrunkelsven (BE), ãEgotripperã; and Boost Producties (NL), ãWaterqueens.ã Photo by Willeke Machiels
Caz Egelie (NL), ãEat me/feed me, help me/need me.ã Photo by Willeke Machiels
ATM Model Art (NL), ãWãatlandis.ã Photo by Ben Nienhuis

schema.headline: A Parade of Floating Artworks Honors Hieronymus Bosch in the Netherlands

title: Artemis II Captures Spectacular Images of 'Earthset' from Deep Space
description: For the first time in more than 50 years, NASA launched a mission to the Moon. A lot has changed since 1972, when we last checked in on the enormo
body:
For the first time in more than 50 years, NASA launched a mission to the Moon. A lot has changed since 1972, when we last checked in on the enormous, rocky satellite, but there is much to learnãand revisitãwhen it comes to traveling through deep space and considering what, as NASA describes it, a ãlong-term returnã to our lunar companion could look like. The Artemis II mission, which is currently underway and scheduled to last a total of 10 days, has also released some remarkable images of our home planet.
A striking image of the Earth ãsettingã behind the cratered Moon takes a truly unique view of our planet and prompts us to consider our perspective. Itãs reminiscent of one of the most iconic photographs of all time, known as ãEarthrise,ã which was snapped by astronaut William Anders during the 1968 Apollo 8 missionãthe first crewed trip to the Moon.
The Earth setting beyond the Moon
Artemis II is scheduled to return to Earth on the evening of April 10, when the crew will splash down into the Pacific Ocean off the California coast.
The Moonãs cratered surface
The far side of the Moon

schema.headline: Artemis II Captures Spectacular Images of 'Earthset' from Deep Space
